mnemonics.title: Exception Hierarchy Tree
mnemonics.content: TEE-CR (Throwable > Error/Exception > Checked/Runtime). Remember: 'Two Exceptional Elephants Can't Race' - Throwable has Error and Exception branches, Exception has Checked and Runtime subclasses

mnemonics.title: Catch Order Rule
mnemonics.content: SPECIFIC before GENERAL (Most specific exception types first in catch blocks). Think: 'Catch the Mouse before the Elephant'

mnemonics.title: Try-with-Resources Syntax
mnemonics.content: ACT (AutoCloseable Comes after Try). Syntax: try(Resource declaration) { ... }

mnemonics.title: SQLException Methods
mnemonics.content: CSM (Code, SQLState, Message). Remember: 'Check SQL Messages' for getErrorCode(), getSQLState(), getMessage()

acronyms.usage: Reverse order for closing JDBC resources: Close ResultSet first, then Statement, then Connection
Automatic resource management syntax introduced in Java 7
Three main exception categories: Checked (must handle), Error (system failures), Unchecked (RuntimeExceptions)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hot potato being thrown up a call stack until someone catches it
Picture a sandwich: try (bread), catch (fillings), finally (bottom bread that always comes last)
Checked exceptions have luggage tags (must be carried), Unchecked are free to roam
Stack of plates: Last opened (ResultSet) on top, first to close, then Statement, then Connection (bottom plate)

analogies.analogy: Like a fire drill - normal operations (try), emergency handling (catch), post-incident cleanup (finally)
A self-cleaning oven - automatically handles cleanup after cooking (execution)
Mandatory insurance - compiler forces you to handle potential issues before driving (executing)
A game of hot potato - methods keep throwing exceptions up the chain until someone catches it
